関連科目: Courses on geopolitics, international law, diplomacy/foreign policy of different countries or regions, global studies, global issues, international cooperation
授業の概要・背景:
1. General introductions, definitions
2. IR theories, relevance of history, global political economy and domestic politics for the analysis of international relations events and developments 
3. History of IR - World War I+II, Cold War, post-Cold War unipolar period, current shift in geopolitics
4. Today's world's most pressing global issues and international cooperation/global governance, policy frameworks etc.
5. Analysis of past and present international relations events
授業方法:
This course will be conducted as a mix of lectures and interaction (seminar/discussion) format. Typically, the topics of each session will be introduced in lecture format (40-50 minutes) and further developed through Q&A/discussions as well as group work. For the duration of the course, students will follow international relations events and developments for discussion/analysis at the beginning of each class.NOTE:This is an 'intensive course" which will be held from February 15 to 19, 2027.
Day 1: February 15th (Mon) 2nd - 3rd; during the 4th period, the students will watch a series of online videos and chose their mini-research topics for presentation in class on Day 5 (Friday)
Day 2: February 16th (Tue) 1st - 3rd
Day 3: February 17th (Wed) 1st - 3rdDay 4: February 18th (Thu) 1st - 3rdDay 5: February 19th (Fri) 1st - 3rd

第1回:
Day 1: February 15th (Mon) 2nd 【Core Weeks 1】《90 minutes》"Face-to-face class"
Introduction of the course, its scope, basic outline, key concepts and definitions
This session will also explain the work that is expected from the students in terms of reading, preparing group discussions, following international news during the course, writing short reflections on selected topics/ reading materials as well as the students' own research projects (presentation in class and final course paper).

On day 1, students should come prepared to:
- introduce themselves briefly including their academic interest in international relations, and
- talk about current major world news, international relations events and dynamics.
第2回:
Day 1: February 15th (Mon) 3rd 【Core Weeks 1】《90 minutes》"Face-to-face class"
International Relations Theory 1:
Introduction: Why do we need IR theory?
The History of Realist and Liberalist Thought
Other IR theories
Post-session homework (reading assignment will be announced at the 1st session of the day for the next day) - time required max. 1-2 hours
第3回:
Day 1: February 15th (Mon) 4th 【Flex Hours】《90 minutes》"Online class（Task-oriented format）"
Introduction to the Mini-Research the students will undertake to prepare for a short presentation in class on Day 5 (Friday): choice of topic, formulating a smart research statement/question or hypothesis, use of basic IR theory and analytical concepts, outline of methodology for the research, finding relevant sources

As this is an intensive course, the session may be offered in class instead of online or as a pre-recorded video for the students to watch at their own pace. Following the introduction, the students will then decide on their topics, work out their approach to this research and identify relevant sources (books, articles, web resources).
第4回:
Day 2: February 16th (Tue) 1st 【Core Weeks 1】《90 minutes》"Face-to-face class"
International Relations Theory 2:
Practice - Understanding International Relations Theory through past and present IR events/dynamics
第5回:
Day 2: February 16th (Tue) 2nd 【Core Weeks 1】《90 minutes》"Face-to-face class"
International Relations Theory 3:
What else do we need to analyze IR?
Post-session homework (reading assignment will be announced at the 1st session of the day for the next day) - time required max. 1-2 hours
第6回:
Day 2: February 16th (Tue) 3rd 【Core Weeks 1】《90 minutes》"Face-to-face class"
International Relations Analysis - Dimensions and Levels of Interaction between States and Other Entities 1:
Analysis Tools and Dimensions of IR, Geopolitics, Geoeconomics, History, Political Geography.
第7回:
Day 3: February 17th (Wed) 1st 【Core Weeks 1】《90 minutes》"Face-to-face class"
International Relations Analysis - Dimensions and Levels of Interaction between States and Other Entities 2:
IR Actors and Levels of Interaction, Diplomatic Protocol and 'Ladder of Options' in IR
第8回:
Day 3: February 17th (Wed) 2nd 【Core Weeks 1】《90 minutes》"Face-to-face class"
International Relations Analysis - Dimensions and Levels of Interaction between States and Other Entities 3:
Practice Session with case studies and discussions
Post-session homework (reading assignment will be announced at the 1st session of the day for the next day) - time required max. 1-2 hours
第9回:
Day 3: February 17th (Wed) 3rd 【Core Weeks 1】《90 minutes》"Face-to-face class"
Understanding Japan’s International Relations and Foreign Policy at the global and regional levels.
第10回:
Day 4: February 18th (Thu) 1st 【Core Weeks 1】《90 minutes》"Face-to-face class"
History of International Relations 1: General introduction, major eras/periods, their IR highlights and major IR events
第11回:
Day 4: February 18th (Thu) 2nd 【Core Weeks 1】《90 minutes》"Face-to-face class"
History of International Relations 2: World Wars 1+2 from an international relations perspective
Post-session homework (reading assignment will be announced at the 1st session of the day for the next day) - time required max. 1-2 hours
第12回:
Day 4: February 18th (Thu) 3rd 【Core Weeks 1】《90 minutes》"Face-to-face class"
History of International Relations 3: The Cold War 1950s-1990s incl. Korean War, Cuban missile crisis, Vietnam War, Afghanistan etc. from an international relations perspective
第13回:
Day 5: February 19th (Fri) 1st 【Core Weeks 1】《90 minutes》"Face-to-face class"
History of International Relations 4: The Current Challenges to the Existing World Order and Their Impact on Today’s International Relations: The Emergence of China, Russia’s Neo-Imperialist Ambitions, Emerging Powers etc., Changing World Order
第14回:
Day 5: February 19th (Fri) 2nd 【Core Weeks 1】《90 minutes》"Face-to-face class"
International Relations and Diplomacy around Today's Global Issues: Peace and Security, Climate Change, Global Health, Global Migration/Displacement, Human Rights, Poverty/Development, Global Trade, Cyber-Security and the Threat of Artificial Intelligence, etc.)
第15回:
Day 5: February 19th (Fri) 3rd 【Core Weeks 1】《90 minutes》"Face-to-face class"
Course Conclusion: The State of International Relations Today and Future Outlook: A New World Order?
Final Discussion. Final Test.
トピック.授業計画1:
Typically, the sessions' topic will be introduced by the course lecturer in a 40-50 minute presentation followed by Q&A, discussions and group work (mini-assignments in class or based on the assigned daily homework/short readings). 
At the beginning of each day of this intensive course, we will look at the previous day's main international news/recent developments. This requires the students to follow the news on a daily basis in print/online media, or TV/radio.
Students will also chose any IR-related topic (IR event, dynamics/development) for their own mini-research to present on the last day of the course.
トピック.授業外学習2: Reading materials and some homework assignments will be given for every day from Day 1 to Day 4 of this course. Additional information will be provided through the LUNA system.

教科書/Required texts0: The books listed above will be held on reserve at the KGU library and/or the SIS library that is located on the second floor of the G Building.  -  Additional sources: other relevant articles and reading materials for this course will be provided in class for each session.
教科書/Required texts1: International Relations: A Very Short Introduction
教科書/Required texts3: Karen A. Mingst, Heather Elko McKibben, etc.
W. W. Norton and the Company
教科書/Required texts4: Introduction to International Relations: Theory and Practice
参考文献・資料/Reference books0: There are no additional reference books other than those listed under "Required Texts" above.  Please see more materials and references/links below (under "Syllabus additional information/シラバス付加情報").
学位授与の方針との関連Relationship to Diploma Polic: All "Learning Goals" listed above directly relate to the Diploma Policy.

Given the nature of this intensive course, there will only be short out-of-class assignments which will be evaluated and graded in LUNA. For in-class group work, feedback will be provided on the spot.
成績評価Grading0: 評価基準等　Grading Criteria etc.
成績評価Grading2: 授業外学修課題／Assignment, homework required outside class (06)
Daily short assignments based on the lectures
成績評価Grading3: 授業への参加度（自発性、積極性、主体性、等）／In-class participation,contribution (09)
Presence and participation in class discussions
成績評価Grading4: 備考　Note Rubrics will be attached to assignments in LUNA where applicable. All evaluation criteria will also be explained in class in detail.
備考Note0: Some essential readings - in addition to the above 'Required Texts" are listed below. Additional relevant reading materials and links to web-articles may be provided in class and through LUNA on an ad-hoc basis.
備考Note1: Political economy analysis is an essential tool for understanding international relations.
備考Note2: Conflict and Cooperation in IR - www.e-ir.info acc231020.pdf
This article talks about the two opposite tendencies in IR: conflict and cooperation.
備考Note3: Realism is one of the two main international relations theories. This article provides a basic explanation of realism in IR.
備考Note4: Realism in IR - ND Int'lSecCenter acc230423.pdf
Realism is one of the two main international relations theories. This article provides a basic explanation of realism in IR.
備考Note5: Liberalism is the second of the two main international relations theories. This article provides a basic explanation of liberalism in IR.
